note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries (<=~95 chars). Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-08-03-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono f32 PCM, numpy rfft): the Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 178.436s but the ffprobed master is 178.051s (0.39s off, past the +-0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 12 blocks concatenated with an exact 0.400s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.03s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset; final end = ffprobed master duration 178.051s.

phrases.text: This is where every damage claim and injury report lands.
One list, under Operations.
When a customer calls about a scratch from the tunnel,
or a manager reports someone hurt on site, the record lives right here.
Know this screen and you can pull up any incident without hunting through menus.
Five columns run across the top.
Report number, type, location, date, status.
Read them left to right and you have the whole story of an incident
before you open a single record.
Look at the first column.
Every report number starts with I-R, then a short code for what happened.
The code is not decoration.
It tells you the kind of incident at a glance, before you read anything else.
Now the type column, and this is the part to memorize.
Five kinds of report, five codes.
Customer Injury is C-I.
Employee Injury, E-I.
Property Damage is P.
Vehicle Damage, V.
And wash equipment damage carries W-E-D.
Every incident you log is one of these five.
Over on the right, the status.
Most reports sit at Open.
One here reads Under Review.
That is the report's place in its lifecycle,
so a quick scan of this column tells you what still needs attention.
Third column, the location code.
Each report is tagged to the site where it happened,
shown as a short code like the ones in this list.
On a support call, that tag tells you which wash and which manager you are dealing with.
Then the date.
Written year first, month, then day,
so the column sorts cleanly and reads the same for everyone.
It is the day the incident occurred, not the day someone typed it up.
Say the list is hundreds long, not five.
The search box up top takes a report number or a keyword and narrows the table as you type.
When a customer gives you a claim number, this is your fastest way in.
Next to it, two more tools.
Filters opens options to narrow the list, and the Search button runs what you have entered.
We are not stepping through the filter choices here.
At the bottom, a count.
Showing one through five of five records, on a single page.
On a busy site, this footer and its page arrows are how you work through the backlog.
Top right sits New Report.
That is how a fresh incident gets started, and it opens by asking for the report type.
On camera we open it to show the choices, then cancel straight back out.
We file nothing today.
So the incident log, in one screen.
Five report types, each with its own code.
A status that shows what is still open.
Search and filters for when the list runs long.
Picture the calls: a scratched car is Vehicle Damage,
an employee who slips is Employee Injury, and both are logged right here.
Learn the five types now, because on a support call you will be naming them from memory.
